source_text: The water cycle describes how water moves through Earth's systems. Heat from the Sun causes liquid water in oceans, lakes, and rivers to evaporate into water vapor. Plants also release water vapor through transpiration. As water vapor rises, it cools and condenses into tiny droplets that form clouds. When the droplets become large and heavy enough, water falls back to Earth as precipitation such as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Some water soaks into the ground, some flows over land as runoff, and some collects again in bodies of water. The cycle continues because Earth's gravity and the Sun's energy keep water moving.
must_keep_facts: The Sun's heat causes evaporation.
Plants release water vapor through transpiration.
Water vapor cools and condenses into clouds.
Precipitation can be rain, snow, sleet, or hail.
Gravity and solar energy keep the cycle moving.

rewrites.below:
The water cycle is the way water moves on Earth. The Sun heats water in oceans, lakes, and rivers. Some of the water evaporates into water vapor. Plants also release water vapor. As the vapor rises, it cools and condenses into clouds. When the drops get large enough, precipitation falls as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Then water can soak into the ground, flow as runoff, or collect again. The Sun and gravity keep the cycle going.

Key Concepts Preserved: water evaporates, condenses, and falls as precipitation.
rewrites.on:
The water cycle describes how water moves through Earth. Heat from the Sun causes water in oceans, lakes, and rivers to evaporate into water vapor, and plants add water vapor through transpiration. As the vapor rises, it cools and condenses into droplets that form clouds. When the droplets become large enough, precipitation falls as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Some water soaks into the ground, some flows over land as runoff, and some collects again in bodies of water. The cycle continues because the Sun's energy and gravity keep water moving.

Key Concepts Preserved: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, and collection.
rewrites.above: The water cycle is a continuous Earth system powered by solar energy and gravity. Solar heating drives evaporation from oceans, lakes, and rivers, while plants contribute additional water vapor through transpiration. As this vapor rises into cooler air, it condenses into tiny droplets that form clouds. Once those droplets become sufficiently large, precipitation returns water to Earth as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. The fallen water may infiltrate soil, travel as surface runoff, or collect in bodies of water, where the cycle begins again. Together, the Sun's energy and Earth's gravity sustain this repeating movement of water.

source_text: An ecosystem is made of living things and nonliving parts of the environment that interact with each other. Producers such as plants make their own food using sunlight. Consumers get energy by eating plants or other animals. Decomposers, including fungi and many bacteria, break down dead organisms and waste. This recycling returns nutrients to the soil and helps support new plant growth. Changes to one part of an ecosystem can affect many other parts because the organisms depend on one another and on resources such as water, air, sunlight, and shelter.
must_keep_facts: An ecosystem includes living and nonliving parts.
Producers make food using sunlight.
Consumers get energy by eating plants or animals.
Decomposers break down dead matter and waste.
Changes in one part of an ecosystem can affect other parts.

rewrites.below:
An ecosystem has living things and nonliving parts. Plants are producers. They use sunlight to make food. Consumers get energy by eating plants or animals. Decomposers, such as fungi and bacteria, break down dead matter and waste. This puts nutrients back into the soil and helps new plants grow. If one part changes, other parts can change too.

Key Concepts Preserved: living and nonliving parts; producers use sunlight; consumers eat; decomposers recycle nutrients.
rewrites.on:
An ecosystem includes living things and nonliving parts of the environment. Plants are producers because they use sunlight to make food. Consumers get energy by eating plants or other animals. Decomposers, such as fungi and bacteria, break down dead organisms and waste and return nutrients to the soil. Those nutrients help new plants grow. Because the parts of an ecosystem depend on one another, a change in one part can affect many other parts.

Key Concepts Preserved: living and nonliving parts interact; producers use sunlight; consumers eat; decomposers return nutrients.
rewrites.above: An ecosystem is a network of interactions between living organisms and the nonliving environment around them. Producers, including plants, capture sunlight to make their own food, while consumers obtain energy by eating plants or other animals. Decomposers such as fungi and many bacteria break down dead organisms and waste, recycling nutrients back into the soil. This nutrient cycling supports future plant growth and keeps the system functioning. Because ecosystem members depend on one another and on resources like water, air, sunlight, and shelter, a change to one component can ripple through the entire ecosystem.

source_text: Cells are the basic units of structure and function in living things. Most cells contain a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and genetic material. In plant cells, a cell wall provides support, and chloroplasts capture light energy for photosynthesis. Animal cells do not have cell walls or chloroplasts. The nucleus stores DNA and helps control the cell's activities. Mitochondria release usable energy from food. Although plant and animal cells have differences, both types carry out the processes needed for life.
must_keep_facts: Cells are the basic units of structure and function in living things.
Most cells have a membrane, cytoplasm, and genetic material.
Plant cells have cell walls and chloroplasts.
Animal cells do not have cell walls or chloroplasts.
The nucleus stores DNA and mitochondria release usable energy from food.

rewrites.below:
Cells are the small parts that make up living things. Most cells have a membrane, cytoplasm, and genetic material. Plant cells also have a cell wall and chloroplasts. Animal cells do not have cell walls or chloroplasts. The nucleus stores DNA and helps control the cell. Mitochondria release energy from food. Both plant and animal cells do the jobs needed for life.

Key Concepts Preserved: cells are basic units; plant cells have walls and chloroplasts; animal cells do not.
rewrites.on:
Cells are the basic units of structure and function in living things. Most cells have a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and genetic material. Plant cells also have a cell wall for support and chloroplasts for photosynthesis. Animal cells do not have cell walls or chloroplasts. The nucleus stores DNA and helps control cell activities, and mitochondria release usable energy from food. Even though plant and animal cells are different, both carry out the processes needed for life.

Key Concepts Preserved: cells are basic units; plant cells have walls and chloroplasts; animal cells do not; nucleus stores DNA.
rewrites.above: Cells are the basic units of structure and function in all living organisms. Most cells include a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and genetic material. Plant cells have additional structures, including a rigid cell wall for support and chloroplasts that capture light energy for photosynthesis, while animal cells lack both cell walls and chloroplasts. The nucleus stores DNA and helps regulate cellular activities, and mitochondria release usable energy from food. Although plant and animal cells differ in structure, both perform the essential processes required for life.

source_text: Weather describes the conditions of the atmosphere at a particular time and place. Scientists measure factors such as temperature, humidity, wind speed, air pressure, and precipitation to describe weather. Weather can change quickly because air masses move and interact. A front forms when two air masses meet. Warm fronts and cold fronts often bring different kinds of weather changes. Meteorologists use data from weather stations, satellites, radar, and computer models to make forecasts, but forecasts are not always exact because the atmosphere is complex.
must_keep_facts: Weather describes atmospheric conditions at a certain time and place.
Weather is described using measurements like temperature, humidity, wind, air pressure, and precipitation.
Weather can change quickly when air masses move and interact.
A front forms when two air masses meet.
Meteorologists use data and models to make forecasts, but forecasts are not always exact.

rewrites.below:
Weather is what the air is like at a certain time and place. Scientists measure temperature, humidity, wind speed, air pressure, and precipitation to describe it. Weather can change quickly when air masses move and interact. A front forms when two air masses meet. Warm fronts and cold fronts often bring different weather changes. Meteorologists use weather stations, satellites, radar, and computer models to make forecasts. Forecasts are not always exact because the atmosphere is complex.

Key Concepts Preserved: scientists measure weather; fronts form when air masses meet; forecasts use data.
rewrites.on:
Weather describes the condition of the atmosphere at a certain time and place. Scientists measure temperature, humidity, wind speed, air pressure, and precipitation to describe weather. Weather can change quickly because air masses move and interact. A front forms when two air masses meet, and warm fronts and cold fronts often bring different kinds of weather changes. Meteorologists use data from weather stations, satellites, radar, and computer models to make forecasts, but forecasts are not always exact because the atmosphere is complex.

Key Concepts Preserved: weather uses measured data; fronts form when air masses meet; forecasts are not perfect.
rewrites.above: Weather refers to atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place. Scientists describe it using measurements such as temperature, humidity, wind speed, air pressure, and precipitation. Because air masses move and interact, weather can change rapidly. A front forms where two air masses meet, and warm fronts and cold fronts often lead to different weather patterns. Meteorologists rely on data from weather stations, satellites, radar, and computer models to produce forecasts. Even so, forecasts are not always exact because the atmosphere is a highly complex system.

source_text: Energy is the ability to cause change or do work. Energy can exist in many forms, including thermal, chemical, electrical, light, and motion energy. Energy can be transferred from one object to another and can also be transformed from one form into another. For example, a flashlight changes chemical energy stored in a battery into electrical energy and then into light and thermal energy. According to the law of conservation of energy, energy is not created or destroyed, but it can change form or move between systems.
must_keep_facts: Energy is the ability to cause change or do work.
Energy exists in multiple forms such as thermal, chemical, electrical, light, and motion.
Energy can be transferred and transformed.
A flashlight changes battery chemical energy into electrical, light, and thermal energy.
Energy is not created or destroyed.

rewrites.below:
Energy is the ability to cause change or do work. It can be thermal, chemical, electrical, light, or motion energy. Energy can move from one object to another. It can also change from one form into another. A flashlight changes chemical energy in a battery into electrical energy and then into light and thermal energy. Energy is not created or destroyed, but it can move or change form.

Key Concepts Preserved: energy can transfer and transform; flashlights change energy forms.
rewrites.on:
Energy is the ability to cause change or do work. It exists in many forms, including thermal, chemical, electrical, light, and motion energy. Energy can be transferred from one object to another, and it can also be transformed from one form into another. For example, a flashlight changes chemical energy in a battery into electrical energy and then into light and thermal energy. According to the law of conservation of energy, energy is not created or destroyed, even though it can change form or move between systems.

Key Concepts Preserved: energy has many forms; energy can transfer and transform; it is not created or destroyed.
rewrites.above: Energy is the ability to cause change or perform work. It exists in multiple forms, including thermal, chemical, electrical, light, and motion energy. Energy may be transferred between objects and also transformed from one form into another. A flashlight provides a clear example by converting chemical energy in its battery into electrical energy, which then becomes light and thermal energy. Under the law of conservation of energy, energy is not created or destroyed; instead, it changes form or moves between systems.

source_text: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. Matter is made of tiny particles called atoms. The three common states of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. In a solid, particles are packed closely and mainly vibrate in place. In a liquid, particles are still close together but can slide past one another. In a gas, particles are much farther apart and move freely. Changes in temperature can cause matter to change state, but the type of matter remains the same during a physical change.
must_keep_facts: Matter has mass and takes up space.
Matter is made of atoms.
The common states of matter are solid, liquid, and gas.
Particles behave differently in solids, liquids, and gases.
Temperature changes can change state without changing the type of matter in a physical change.

rewrites.below:
Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. Matter is made of tiny particles called atoms. The main states of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. In a solid, particles stay close together and vibrate in place. In a liquid, particles stay close but can slide past each other. In a gas, particles are farther apart and move freely. Changes in temperature can make matter change state, but the type of matter stays the same in a physical change.

Key Concepts Preserved: matter has mass and takes up space; solids, liquids, and gases are states of matter.
rewrites.on:
Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space, and it is made of tiny particles called atoms. The three common states of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. In solids, particles are packed closely and mostly vibrate in place. In liquids, particles stay close together but can slide past one another. In gases, particles are farther apart and move freely. Changes in temperature can cause matter to change state, but the type of matter stays the same during a physical change.

Key Concepts Preserved: atoms make matter; particle motion differs in solids, liquids, and gases; physical changes keep the same matter.
rewrites.above: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space, and it is composed of tiny particles called atoms. The three common states of matter are solid, liquid, and gas. In solids, particles are tightly packed and primarily vibrate in place. In liquids, particles remain close together while moving past one another. In gases, particles are much farther apart and move freely. Temperature changes can cause matter to change state, but during a physical change the substance remains the same type of matter.
